_description: All cultural content entry types. Every type lives in the same 'cultural_entries' collection, distinguished by 'content_type'. Shared base fields appear on every document; type-specific fields are nested under 'type_data'. This keeps queries simple — filter the whole archive by content_type — while keeping type-specific schemas clean.

base_fields._note: Every cultural entry document, regardless of type, carries all of these fields.

locality._note: How the entry is placed in the hierarchy. country_id always required. Everything else as specific as the contributor can be. Use 'unknown' strings where unspecified.

community_id.note: null if community is unknown — valid

community_unknown.note: True when contributor cannot identify the community. Distinct from null community_id — this is an explicit 'I don't know'.

locality_notes.note: Free text to clarify locality when the hierarchy doesn't capture it. e.g. 'Practiced in the Kibera Luo diaspora, not the Nyanza homeland'

description.note: The primary text body in the original language. Minimum 50 characters.

visibility_reason.note: Required if visibility is restricted or sealed

on_chain_ref.note: Cardano transaction hash — null until anchored

last_activity_at.note: Updated on new comment, relation, or media — used to surface active entries

type_data.note: Type-specific fields. Schema differs per content_type. See definitions below.

oral_tradition._description: Oral histories, origin narratives, fables, proverbs, creation myths. A story always belongs to a community. Multiple versions of the same story coexist.

body.note: The story itself, in original language

occasion.note: When is this told? e.g. 'Fireside at night', 'Initiation season'

narrator_type.note: Who traditionally tells this? e.g. 'Elders only', 'Grandmothers to grandchildren'

is_restricted.note: Some stories may only be told in certain contexts or to certain people

example.title: Mwambu na Sela — Kuanzia
example.description: Mwambu na Sela ni hadithi ya asili ya Waluhya...
example.description_english: Mwambu and Sela is the Luhya origin story, recounting the first man and woman placed at the top of Mount Elgon...

type_data.body: Mwambu na Sela balikuwa watu wa kwanza...
type_data.body_english: Mwambu and Sela were the first people placed by Were (God) at the summit of Mount Elgon...
type_data.occasion: Told by elders around the fire, especially during initiation seasons
type_data.narrator_type: Elders only — specifically male elders to male initiates
type_data.moral_or_meaning: Establishes the Luhya relationship with the land and with Mount Elgon as an ancestral home

food_tradition._description: Food and drink traditions with cultural context. Community is optional — food can be documented as broadly as 'Kenyan' or as specifically as 'Bukusu ceremonial'.

who_prepares.note: Is preparation gendered, age-specific, or role-specific?

ingredient_ids.note: Links to ingredient entries in the archive

what_is_being_lost.note: What knowledge around this food is disappearing in urban settings?

example.title: Ingokho — Kuku wa Kienyeji
example.title_english: Ingokho — Traditional chicken as cultural symbol
example.description: Ingokho si chakula tu — ni ishara ya heshima na ukarimu miongoni mwa Waluhya...
example.description_english: Ingokho (traditional chicken) is not simply food among the Luhya — it is the foremost symbol of hospitality and respect. To slaughter ingokho for a guest signals the highest honour...

type_data.occasion: When guests arrive, especially significant guests or in-laws
type_data.who_prepares: Traditionally prepared by women of the household
type_data.preparation_notes: The chicken is slaughtered and prepared fresh — not frozen. The gizzard is reserved for the male head of the household or honoured elder.
type_data.what_is_being_lost: In urban Nairobi, the practice of specifically seeking out free-range chicken and the ritual significance of offering it are giving way to broiler chicken with no ceremonial weight.

ingredient._description: Indigenous plants, animals, fungi, and materials used in food, medicine, or craft. Often cross-community — attaches at region or country level.

name_indigenous.note: Name in the original language — this is the primary record

example.title: Mrenda

locality.locality_notes: Grown and eaten across western Kenya — Luhya, Luo, and Teso communities all use mrenda. Not specific to one group.

example.description: Mrenda ni mboga ya majani inayolimwa sana magharibi mwa Kenya...
example.description_english: Mrenda (Corchorus olitorius, jute mallow) is a dark leafy vegetable widely grown in western Kenya. It is slimy when cooked and typically prepared with bicarbonate of soda to reduce the sliminess. A staple accompaniment to ugali.

type_data.season: Rainy season — grows best during long and short rains
type_data.where_it_grows: Home gardens, small farms throughout western Kenya and along the Lake Victoria basin
type_data.harvesting_knowledge: Leaves picked by hand from young plants. Older plants become too fibrous. Usually harvested in the morning before the heat of the day.

music_tradition._description: Musical traditions, drum patterns, songs, lullabies, work songs. Audio is the primary record.

who_performs.note: Is performance restricted by gender, age, or role?

instruments_used.note: Instrument names — cross-referenced to instrument entries

example.title: Isukuti — Ngoma ya Waluhya
example.description: Isukuti ni mfumo wa ngoma unaohusisha ngoma tatu za ukubwa tofauti...
example.description_english: Isukuti is an interlocking three-drum tradition using drums of different sizes — the large lichiche, the medium isikulu, and the small kachicha — played together to create layered polyrhythmic patterns.

type_data.occasion: Imbalu circumcision ceremony, weddings, harvest celebrations, cultural festivals
type_data.who_performs: Traditionally performed by men. Women and children may dance to it but do not play the drums.
type_data.rhythm_notes: The three drums interlock in a call-and-response pattern. The large lichiche sets the base. The kachicha plays the fastest pattern.

instrument._description: Traditional musical instruments — construction, who plays them, their occasions.

example.title: Nyatiti
example.description: Nyatiti en thum mar Joluo ma en gi tende aboro...
example.description_english: The nyatiti is an eight-string lyre of the Luo people, played with metal finger rings on the right hand and a metal toe ring on the left foot that strikes a bell attached to the instrument's body.

type_data.materials: Hardwood frame, animal hide soundboard, gut strings traditionally (now often nylon or wire), metal rings for playing
type_data.construction_notes: The body is carved from a single piece of hardwood. Historically a specialised craft passed within certain families.
type_data.who_plays: Traditionally played by men, often professional musicians (jogi). Playing while walking was a mark of skill.
type_data.pitch_or_tuning: Pentatonic tuning, varies by player tradition

ceremony._description: Ceremonies, rites of passage, seasonal rituals. Always community-bound. Visibility defaults and community council control are critical here.

participants.note: Who takes part? Who may witness?

example.title: Imbalu — Tohara ya Babukusu
example.title_english: Imbalu — the Bukusu male circumcision ceremony
example.description: Imbalu ni sherehe ya tohara inayofanyika kila mwaka wenye nambari ya jozi...
example.description_english: Imbalu is the Bukusu male circumcision ceremony held every even year. It is among the most significant cultural events in western Kenya, drawing participants and observers from across the Bukusu diaspora.
example.visibility_reason: Detailed ritual content is governed by the Bukusu community council. Public entry contains overview only.

type_data.when_it_occurs: Every even year — August. The next ceremony is 2026.
type_data.duration: The full process spans several months from preparation through healing
type_data.participants: Bukusu boys and young men of initiation age. Community members gather as witnesses. Full community participation.
type_data.site: Family homesteads and community gathering grounds in Bungoma County
type_data.restriction_note: Specific ritual details, sacred songs, and inner ceremony content are sealed. This entry contains community-approved overview only.

craft_technique._description: Traditional making techniques. The most free-standing type — does not require a community. Connects to Layer 2 marketplace artisan profiles.

learning_method.note: How is this knowledge transmitted? e.g. 'Mother to daughter', 'Guild apprenticeship'

marketplace_link.note: Direct link to a Canopy marketplace seller practising this technique

example.title: Uusaji wa Kikoi — Pwani ya Kenya

locality.locality_notes: Practiced across the Swahili coast communities — Mombasa, Malindi, Lamu. Cross-community craft.

example.description: Kikoi ni kitambaa cha mapambo kilichosukwa kwenye mikono ya wavulana...
example.description_english: Kikoy weaving is a centuries-old Swahili coast textile tradition producing the distinctive fringed cotton wrap worn by men and used across households as towel, garment, and gift.

type_data.who_practises: Historically men — weaving on the Swahili coast was a male profession unlike in many other African traditions
type_data.learning_method: Apprenticeship — young men learn from established weavers, typically within the family trade
type_data.technique_steps: Cotton warped on a pit loom. Weft threads passed by hand shuttle. Pattern created by variation in colour sequence. Fringe knotted by hand at the cut ends.

clothing_tradition._description: Clothing, dress traditions, and body adornment. Usually community-specific. Some patterns carry restricted knowledge.

example.title: Shuka ya Kimasai
example.title_english: Maasai shuka — the iconic red cloth
example.description: Shuka ni nguo ya msingi ya Wamasai, inayojulikana kwa rangi yake nyekundu...
example.description_english: The shuka is the foundational garment of the Maasai, recognisable worldwide for its red colour and check pattern. It is worn as a toga-style wrap and carries status, age-grade, and ceremonial significance through its colours and how it is worn.

type_data.occasion: Daily wear and all ceremonial occasions
type_data.who_wears: All Maasai — colour and pattern signal age-grade, marital status, and gender. Warriors (moran) typically wear solid red. Elders wear blue or mixed.
materials: Cotton cloth (historically wool or animal hide)
Natural dyes — red from ochre earth, blue from indigo
type_data.pattern_meaning: Solid red: warrior age-grade. Blue and red check: elder men. Pink and purple tones: women and young girls. The pattern itself is not restricted but its wearing context carries meaning.

language_entry._description: Language and dialect documentation. Can be a full language overview, a vocabulary collection, or a specific dialect record.

dialect_of_id.note: If this is a dialect entry, link to the parent language

example.title: Dholuo
example.description: Dholuo en lep mar Joluo ma nitie yo ho apar....
example.description_english: Dholuo is a Nilotic language spoken by approximately 4 million Luo people in Kenya and Tanzania, belonging to the Western Nilotic branch of the Nilo-Saharan language family.

sample_phrases.meaning: How are you? / How are you going?

place_knowledge._description: Traditional knowledge about sites, rivers, hills, boundaries, and settlements. Primarily geographic rather than tribal — multiple communities can contribute to the same place.

communities_connected.note: All community_ids with a relationship to this place

example.title: Ayany — Ardhi ya Kabila la Nubian
example.title_english: Ayany — the original Nubian land grant area

locality.locality_notes: Kibera — the Ayany area is associated with the original Nubian land grant but is now home to many communities including Luo, Luhya, Kikuyu, and Nubian residents.

example.description: Ayany ni eneo ndogo ndani ya Kibera ambalo lina historia ndefu ya Wanubia...
example.description_english: Ayany is a sub-estate within Kibera with a layered history: the land was originally part of the 1904 land grant to Sudanese (Nubian) soldiers who served under the British colonial administration. The name 'Kibera' itself derives from the Nubian word 'kibra' meaning 'forest' or 'jungle'.

type_data.cultural_significance: The original Nubian settlement gives Kibera its name and its founding community. The Nubian community's land rights here remain contested to this day.
type_data.what_has_changed: Kibera has grown to a population estimated at 250,000-1 million. The original Nubian character of Ayany persists in the mosque, community gathering structures, and family compounds — but is rapidly changing under development pressure.

medicine_knowledge._description: Traditional healing knowledge. Almost always restricted or community_only. Documented with care — the existence of the knowledge is recorded even when the content is sealed.

example.title: Dawa ya Asili ya Joluo — Muhtasari
example.description: Joluo nigi system maduong' mar thieth mar chuny kod ringruok...
example.description_english: The Luo hold an extensive system of plant-based and spiritual healing knowledge. This entry documents that the knowledge exists and is held by identified knowledge holders within the community. Detailed content is sealed at the community council's direction.
example.visibility_reason: Detailed plant use and healing practices are restricted to community members and practising healers. This entry serves as a record of existence and a contact point.

type_data.who_practises: Designated healers (ajuoga) — a role that is hereditary in certain families

person._description: Elders, knowledge holders, artisans, storytellers, and cultural figures. Always requires documented consent.

example.title: Mama Sarah Wekesa
example.title_english: Mama Sarah Wekesa — Luhya elder and story keeper
example.description: Mama Sarah Wekesa ni mzee wa Babukusu...
example.description_english: Mama Sarah Wekesa is a Bukusu elder from Bungoma County, one of the last living practitioners of the full Imbalu song repertoire and a keeper of Luhya origin stories.

type_data.role: Story keeper and Imbalu song holder
type_data.bio: Born in Bungoma County approximately 1948. Mama Sarah has participated in 12 Imbalu cycles and holds the full ceremonial song sequence. She learned from her grandmother, who was herself trained in the late colonial period.
